concerns.text: The spec proposes training a 2B parameter 'Mixture-of-Transformers' world model on a 10% subset of LAION/AudioSet/HowTo100M (approx. 50GB) within a 6-hour wall-clock budget on a 2-core CPU runner. This is scientifically unsound: training a 2B parameter model to convergence on such a massive, heterogeneous dataset requires GPU acceleration and weeks of compute, not 6 hours on 2 CPU cores. The 'validation' of the model's capability is impossible under these constraints, rendering the core research question untestable as designed.

The evaluation design assumes the trained world model can be 'fine-tuned' to a policy network for embodied tasks (Mini-World/Habitat) within the same 6-hour budget. In physical AI, world models (generative/dynamics) and policy networks (control) are distinct objectives. Fine-tuning a 2B model for control on a CPU-only runner is computationally infeasible. The spec conflates 'training a world model' with 'training a policy,' and the proposed hardware constraints make the empirical validation of the research question (omnimodal vs. unimodal success rates) impossible.

The spec validates 'omnimodal representation quality' using cross-modal retrieval (Recall@1/5) on COCO-Cap and AudioCaps. However, the model is trained on LAION, AudioSet, HowTo100M, and RoboSet. While these datasets contain image-text and audio-text pairs, the specific benchmarks (COCO-Cap, AudioCaps) are distinct held-out datasets. The spec fails to account for the domain shift: a model trained on web-scraped video (HowTo100M) and robotic data (RoboSet) may not generalize to standard captioning benchmarks without specific fine-tuning, which is not budgeted. The validation target is not independent of the training distribution's specific characteristics, and the lack of fine-tuning for retrieval makes the 'validation' of fusion quality tenuous.

The spec mandates paired t-tests on 5 random seeds (FR-005, SC-002). With N=5, the statistical power to detect a 5% absolute improvement (SC-001) is extremely low unless the variance is negligible. In embodied AI, success rates often have high variance due to stochastic environments. Relying on a t-test with N=5 to claim 'statistical significance' (p<0.05) for a 5% gain is methodologically weak and likely to yield false negatives, failing to validate the hypothesis even if the omnimodal model is superior.
